common_name: Red Norland Potato (Very Early Smooth Red-Skinned White-Flesh Potato)

planting.note: Plant certified seed potato pieces (each with at least one eye) 4–6 weeks before the last frost in loose, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.8–6.5) soil in full sun; Red Norland is one of the earliest-maturing potato varieties (70–80 days) making it well-suited to the short cool season in zone 9 before summer heat arrives.

directions: Plant certified seed potato pieces 4–6 weeks before the last frost in loose, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Red Norland is one of the finest early-maturing red potato varieties for home garden production — producing attractive, smooth, bright-red-skinned, white-fleshed tubers that mature in only 70–80 days from planting, making it the best choice for Zone 9 gardeners whose spring potato window closes when summer heat arrives. The smooth red skin (without the russeting or rough texture of some red varieties) and excellent appearance make Red Norland outstanding for presentation at harvest — new Red Norland potatoes dug fresh from the garden have bright color and clean appearance. The white flesh is excellent for boiling, steaming, and potato salad — waxy enough to hold its shape when cooked, with a moist, creamy texture and mild potato flavor that excels in salads, soups, and boiled presentations. In zone 9, plant seed potatoes in January–February for a May–June harvest before summer heat makes potato production impractical. Hill soil over the developing plants 2–3 times as they grow to ensure tubers do not green from light exposure. Red Norland has been one of the most widely grown early red potato varieties in American home gardens since its introduction in the 1950s — a reliable, consistent producer of high-quality fresh-eating potatoes.

common_name: Purple Majesty Potato (Deep Purple Skin and Flesh Specialty Potato)

planting.note: Plant certified seed potato pieces 4–6 weeks before the last frost in loose, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.8–6.5) soil in full sun; Purple Majesty retains its vivid purple color best when steamed or microwaved rather than boiled — water-cooking leaches anthocyanins and dilutes the distinctive purple color in the finished dish.

directions: Plant certified seed potato pieces 4–6 weeks before the last frost in loose, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Purple Majesty is a Colorado State University potato introduction producing tubers with dramatic deep-purple skin and equally vivid deep-purple flesh throughout — not a surface tint or partial coloring but consistently, intensely purple from skin to center in every tuber. The purple coloration comes from high concentrations of anthocyanin pigments that are the same class of antioxidants found in blueberries and acai — Purple Majesty has among the highest anthocyanin levels of any potato variety tested, providing both the extraordinary visual quality and the health-attributed benefits associated with anthocyanin-rich foods. The color partially survives light cooking — steamed or microwaved Purple Majesty retains much of its striking purple appearance, while hard-boiled or oven-roasted preparations create dramatic visual presentations unlike any other potato. The flavor is mild and pleasant — comparable to other specialty potatoes — with the skin adding a slightly earthy complexity. Purple Majesty is oblong-shaped with smooth skin and produces well under typical potato growing conditions. In zone 9, plant in January–February for spring harvest. The vivid color makes Purple Majesty outstanding in mixed potato salads where the purple tubers contrast dramatically with red, gold, and white varieties.

common_name: Paul Robeson Tomato (Complex Deep Brick-Red Russian Dark Heirloom)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Paul Robeson is named in honor of the American singer, actor, and civil rights activist Paul Robeson — a Russian heirloom commemorating a figure celebrated in the Soviet Union for his commitment to racial justice.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Paul Robeson is a celebrated Russian heirloom tomato that is consistently rated by connoisseurs as one of the finest-flavored dark tomatoes available — producing medium-large (7–10 oz), round to oblate fruits in a distinctive deep brick-red to mahogany-brownish-purple color with dark-green shoulders that is the hallmark of the 'black' Russian tomato aesthetic. The flavor is remarkable: deeply complex, rich, smoky, and earthy with a balanced sweetness and moderate acidity that creates a dimensional, wine-like flavor profile that many experienced tomato tasters consider superior to any other tomato type. The flavor intensity — often described as the most complex and layered of all dark heirloom tomatoes — makes Paul Robeson outstanding for fresh eating (particularly with nothing more than salt and olive oil), in Caprese-style presentations, and in cooking where the rich, smoky character concentrates further. Paul Robeson was introduced to American seed savers from a Russian collection by Marina Danilenko in the 1990s and has become one of the most respected and sought-after heirlooms in the American seed-saving community. As an open-pollinated heirloom, seeds save true to type. In zone 9, Paul Robeson produces best in the spring growing window (February–June) and requires the long spring season to develop its finest fruit quality.

common_name: Amana Orange Tomato (Very Large Sweet Low-Acid Orange Heirloom Beefsteak)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Amana Orange originated in the Amana Colonies of Iowa, an historic German communal settlement that maintained distinctive traditional varieties; the tomato was preserved by colony members for generations before entering the American heirloom seed trade.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Amana Orange is one of the finest very-large orange beefsteak heirloom tomatoes — producing massive (1–2 lb), irregularly shaped, deeply orange-colored fruits with exceptionally sweet, very low-acid flesh that creates the most mild, sweet, non-acidic fresh tomato experience available from any heirloom type. The very large fruit size and characteristic sweet, almost candy-like low-acid flavor make Amana Orange one of the most distinctive eating experiences among heirloom tomatoes. The brilliant, uniform deep-orange color — consistent across the exterior and interior flesh without the bicolor patterns of Pineapple or Big Rainbow — provides a vivid single-color presentation that is striking in its own right. The meaty, dense orange flesh has relatively few seeds and an exceptionally high flesh-to-gel ratio that maximizes the edible portion. As an open-pollinated heirloom from the Amana Colonies of Iowa, the variety carries cultural significance alongside its outstanding eating quality. Amana Orange plants are large, vigorous indeterminate vines requiring sturdy 6-foot support. The long growing season (80–95 days) means zone 9 gardeners should start seeds as early as possible to ensure fruit development before summer heat. An outstanding choice for anyone who finds standard tomatoes too acid.

common_name: Milk and Wine Crinum (Striped White-and-Pink Fragrant Gulf South Crinum Lily)

planting.note: Plant large bulbs in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Milk and Wine Crinum prefers planting with the neck of the bulb at or just above the soil surface — planting too deep reduces flowering; divide congested clumps every 5–7 years to maintain vigor.

directions: Plant large bulbs in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Milk and Wine Crinum is one of the most beloved and long-lived heirloom bulbs of Gulf South gardens — a heritage Crinum hybrid producing large clusters of fragrant, trumpet-shaped flowers with white petals boldly striped with wine-red to rosy-pink along the midrib of each petal, creating the distinctive 'milk and wine' appearance that gives the variety its evocative name. The large, heavily fragrant flowers (6–8 per umbel, 4–5 inch diameter) are produced in flushes from late spring through fall, typically blooming most heavily after rainfall events similar to the related Zephyranthes rain lilies. The sweet fragrance — particularly intense in the evening — is one of the most distinctive qualities of Crinum lilies generally, and the large flower clusters create an arresting display at the back of the border or as a specimen planting. Milk and Wine Crinum is extremely long-lived and persistent — established clumps in old Gulf South gardens often persist for many decades, growing into very large clumps with dozens of flowering stalks annually without any attention. The massive bulbs (sometimes reaching the size of a football in old, undivided clumps) have few pests and diseases. The handsome, strap-shaped evergreen foliage (3–4 feet long) provides year-round architectural interest. Tolerates periodic flooding, drought, salt spray, and heavy clay soils — one of the most adaptable ornamental bulbs for challenging Gulf South conditions.
